narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 44% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is higher than what we'd typically approve. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the average student's mid-range position—along with a lower LSAT score of 30 compared to the typical 37, a slightly below-average undergraduate GPA of 3.1, and coming from a lower-middle family income bracket. You're a male Asian student planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the majority of applicants in those categories. The model's decision was driven primarily by your third-year school decile, which had the strongest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in importance was your LSAT score, also nudging the outlook toward failure, followed closely by your first-year school decile doing the same. Your family income, racial background, and undergraduate GPA each played supporting roles in tipping the scales toward a higher failure risk, while your gender as male provided a small counterbalance favoring success. Being full-time had a minor push toward failure but wasn't a big factor. Overall, your academic performance in school, especially later on, and your test scores stood out as the key drags compared to typical students who land in higher deciles and score better on the LSAT. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your grades through better study habits or tutoring to climb those school rankings, and seriously consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average. Building a stronger undergraduate record if you're still in school could help too, as these are areas within your control that directly tie to bar success. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile toward the stronger applicants who pass on the first try. Keep pushing—you've got the determination to turn this around.
